title_en: A Dying Poet’s Farewell to the World
title_zh: 一位垂危诗人对世界的告别

paragraphs.en: In his final days, Franz Wright tapped into a rich vein of material, recording dark, beautiful, and self-deprecating poems on tape and in scribbles all over his apartment.
paragraphs.zh: 在他生命最后的日子里，弗兰茨·赖特（Franz Wright）开启了一股丰厚的创作资源，将黑暗、优美且自嘲的诗句写在录音带上，并在公寓里到处乱涂乱写。

paragraphs.en: Writing poetry made the late Franz Wright miserable, but it was an improvement on just about everything else in his life. His mentor in this essentially comic predicament was his estranged father, the poet James Wright. When Franz was fifteen, he sent his dad a seven-line poem. The elder Wright’s response soon arrived from New York City, addressed jauntily from one “good craftsman” to another: “Franz, I am non-plussed,” the younger Wright recalled, in a poem. “I’ll be damned. You can write. What can I say? Welcome to Hell.”
paragraphs.zh: 对已故的弗兰茨·赖特来说，写诗让他很痛苦，但也比他生活中的几乎所有其他事情都要好得多。他在这一出几乎喜剧化的困境中的导师，是与他疏远多年的父亲、诗人詹姆斯·赖特（James Wright）。弗兰茨十五岁时曾给他父亲寄去一首七行诗。年长的赖特很快从纽约回信，像一位“好工匠”写给另一位“好工匠”一样俏皮地写道：弗兰茨回忆说，年轻的赖特在一首诗里写道，“弗兰茨，我很惊讶。” “我他妈的……” “你会写诗，我该说什么呢？欢迎来到地狱。”

paragraphs.en: It was the only home they shared, and Wright took advantage of his father’s hospitality. In fourteen volumes of poems, including “Walking to Martha’s Vineyard,” which won a Pulitzer Prize in 2004, Franz Wright made Hell habitable, almost cheerful. “Axe in Blossom: Last Poems & Fragments” (Knopf), assembled by the poet’s widow, Elizabeth Oehlkers Wright, after his death, in 2015, collects work from the days when Wright was maintaining what John Keats called a “posthumous existence”; dying from lung cancer, he dictated some of these tender, hardboiled, and wisecracking poems into a tape recorder, too weak to write, or left them as voice-mail messages for friends in an “odd-sounding croak that was once a good voice.” Photos taken at Wright’s apartment after his death show lines of verse written on the walls, on the appliances, on coffee filters, and, the most Boston thing evah, carved with a ballpoint into a Styrofoam Dunkin’ Donuts cup. It looks like a crime scene, if writing poetry were a crime. “I feel like Philip Marlowe sometimes,” Wright wrote, lost in a “Los Angeles of words.”
paragraphs.zh: 那其实是他们共同拥有的唯一一个家，弗兰茨充分利用了父亲的好客之恩。在包括《走向马莎花园岛》（Walking to Martha’s Vineyard）在内的十四卷诗集中，这部作品于2004年获得普利策奖，弗兰茨·赖特把“地狱”写得像可以居住、甚至几乎有些欢快。2015年，诗人妻子伊丽莎白·厄尔克斯·赖特（Elizabeth Oehlkers Wright）整理出版的《刀与花：最后的诗与残篇》（Axe in Blossom: Last Poems & Fragments, Knopf）收录了他在约翰·济慈称其为“身后生活”（posthumous existence）时期写下的作品；他因肺癌去世前，已经虚弱到不能书写，于是把其中一些既温柔又硬朗又带讽刺意味的诗句口述进录音机，或以“曾经是好嗓音、如今听上去有些怪异的呱呱声”录下给朋友的语音留言。赖特去世后在他公寓拍下的照片显示，诗句写在墙上、家电上、咖啡过滤纸上，甚至“最有波士顿风格的一幕”——刻在一只一次性聚苯乙烯泡沫的唐恩·唐恩（Dunkin’ Donuts）纸杯上。若写诗是犯罪，这看起来像一个犯罪现场。赖特写道：“有时我觉得自己像菲利普·马洛（Philip Marlowe）”，他曾迷失在“一个由单词构成的洛杉矶”里。

paragraphs.en: As his voracious sentience began to cleave away from his suffering frame, this “facsimile of the person” became more and more a poet of the estranged body: “I am traveling away from my body in widening rings,” he writes, and yet, “If I think I have problems, / I look for the mirror.” Any errand outdoors makes him a kind of zombie Frank O’Hara, clomping down the quiet chain-link and brick-faced blocks near his home in Waltham, Massachusetts. Even a trip six blocks to the post office is an “exceptional event, long planned.” Wright’s spectacular disarray—you worried about him, just running into him on the street—was always first an occasion for self-ridicule: spotting his own reflection on a bus one afternoon, “I looked like a suicide / returning an overdue book to the library.” On the edge of death, though, the scenario becomes nearly slapstick; strangers who come across Wright’s body on the sidewalk move “around him, the way you would / dog shit.”
paragraphs.zh: 当他那近乎贪婪的意识开始与受苦的身体逐渐分离时，这个“人的摹本”越来越像一个与身体疏离的诗人：“我正以越来越大的同心圆离开我的身体，”他写道；但又说，“如果我觉得自己有问题，/ 我就去照镜子看。”任何一次出门都让他成了一个僵尸般的弗兰克·奥哈拉（Frank O’Hara），踩着脚步在他在马萨诸塞州沃尔瑟姆（Waltham）附近家门口那排安静的铁丝网与红砖街区里走来走去。哪怕是步行六个街区去邮局，这也成了“一个例外性的事件，早有计划”。赖特那惊人的凌乱——你会在路上偶遇他，会担心他——最初总是自嘲的开场白：有一次傍晚在公交车上看到自己的倒影，“我看起来就像一个自杀者 / 回去给图书馆归还逾期未还的书。”然而到了生命的边缘，这一幕几乎变成了闹剧；陌生人看到他倒在街边时，会“绕着他走开，像你会绕着/ 狗屎一样”。

paragraphs.en: Wright’s poems are usually at least a little funny, and often work like extended punch lines; the way to read them is to try to guess the elided sick joke. What happens to the body of a lifelong smoker when he dies? In “Involuntary Detox: Smoking Porch,” it keeps smoking, of course, but not in any old crematorium:
paragraphs.zh: 怀特的诗通常至少带点儿幽默感，而且常常像拉长了的笑点来写；阅读时的诀窍就是尝试猜出那句被省略的黑色玩笑。一个长期吸烟者去世后，身体会怎样？在《非自愿戒毒：吸烟门廊》中，它当然还在继续抽烟，但不是在普通的火葬场里。

paragraphs.en: What are you waiting for?
paragraphs.zh: 你在等什么？

paragraphs.en: Then come in. The nicatorium’s all yours.
paragraphs.zh: 那就进来吧。尼卡托里姆，都是你的。

paragraphs.en: Nicatorium! Wright went through many periods of detox, and was institutionalized several times; here he is welcomed by a stock character, the catatonic roommate who promises he hasn’t “said a word / all day, and don’t intend to / now.” Or maybe that’s a bored crematory worker, just punching the clock—or is it God, whose weird, voyeuristic silence troubled Wright into composing some of his most powerful poems? In any case, the smoker gets his wish, and his comeuppance: “Consider yourself completely alone.” That’s a poet’s fantasy, and a child’s darkest fear.
paragraphs.zh: “尼卡托里姆”！怀特曾经历过许多次戒毒期，也因此多次被送进机构；在这里，他受到了一位定型化角色的迎接——这个木僵的室友说他一整天都“一句话没说 / 也完全不打算 / 现在说。”或许那是个厌倦的火葬场工作人员，仅仅在打卡上班；或者是上帝吧，正是那种怪异的偷窥式沉默，让怀特陷入困境，进而写下了他最有力量的几首诗。无论如何，这位吸烟者得到了他的心愿和应有的下场：“把你自己想象成完全孤独。”这既是诗人的幻想，也是孩子最深处的恐惧。

paragraphs.en: In these poems, anticipation is inextricable from dread. Emily Dickinson received “Heavenly Hurt” from the winter “Slant of light” entering her bedroom. As a boy, Wright kept his eye on the door, hoping that the father he idolized would someday return; to maintain this vigil into adulthood is to open oneself up to grace but also to injury. Waking in the night, Wright wonders if there is “someone else / who wakes disturbed, alone; / too bad we can’t talk / on our tiny phone.” In another insomnia poem, “Wait,” Wright keeps watch for the “late light,” which is “always / leaving” even “as it arrives.” For a child, this “steplight” could bring comfort, or it could bring a drunken thrashing. Wright wrote that his father beat him with a belt; his stepfather, he went on, “took over / somewhat more expertly.” If the soul survives, these poems suggest, it’s only by being battered out of the body.
paragraphs.zh: 在这些诗里，期待与恐惧密不可分。艾米丽·狄金森从冬日里那道“斜斜射入她卧室”的“天赋般的痛”中感受到创伤。小时候，赖特始终盯着门，希望自己崇拜的父亲总有一天会回来；而把这种守望持续到成年，就等于让自己既敞开于恩典，也敞开于伤害。夜里醒来时，他在想是否还有“别的人 / 在惊醒时醒着，独自一人；/ 可惜我们不能 / 在这只小手机上交谈。”在另一首失眠诗《等待》里，赖特守着“晚光”，它“总是在 / 来临的同时 / 正在离去。”对一个孩子来说，这道“台阶光”或许带来安慰，或者带来一阵醉醺醺的狂扭。赖特写道，他父亲曾用皮带打他；后来，继父则“更熟练一些”地接过了这件事。若灵魂要得以存续，这些诗似乎在说，唯有被痛打出身体之外才可能做到。

paragraphs.en: “Axe in Blossom” borrows its title from a line in “I Hear,” by the German Romanian poet and Holocaust survivor Paul Celan, who drowned in the Seine in 1970. Celan’s shattered “neversongs” taught Wright important elements of his own style: the lines narrowing as they approach silence, like water disappearing down a drain; the clauses and multisyllabic words split like cordwood by line breaks; the pressurized neologisms picking up where available vocabulary runs out of road; the rich, sometimes asphyxiating details that make a beeline into the senses. In an homage, “Axe in Blossom: To Paul Celan,” the dead are called back to life by the “mere scent of their wives baking bread.” It takes a little more, in fact, than the “mere scent”: it takes an elegiac poetry of sensory nearness that mixes such things as warm bread and burning bodies. Wright is something like a laureate of the putrid, the stale, the acrid, and the moldy, but also of unbidden, stunning adjacent beauty: the “still-daytime / three-quarters moon, / the first snowflakes, swirling,” even as the new day itself is unthinkable.
paragraphs.zh: 《开春的斧头》借用了德语-罗马尼亚裔诗人、浩劫幸存者保罗·策兰《我听见》中的一句作为题名。策兰残缺而破碎的“从不曾”之歌为赖特的个人风格提供了重要元素：行句像落入下水道般向沉默收拢；分句与多音节词在换行处被劈成柴柴；当现成词汇用尽时，充满压力的新造词接棒；以及丰富而有时令人窒息的细节，像一条径直刺向感官的航线。为策兰所作的致敬诗《开春的斧头：致保罗·策兰》里，亡者会被“仅仅她们妻子在烤面包的香味”召回生机。实际上，它需要的远不止“仅仅”的香味：它需要一种挽歌式的感官临近之诗，把温热的面包与燃烧的尸体之类的事物混合在一起。赖特像是脏乱、陈旧、刺鼻与发霉的诗意领唱者；同时也在不经意间领会到惊艳而逼近的美：那“仍在白日的时分 / 三分之四弯月，/ 第一片打旋的雪花，”即便新的一天本身几乎不可想象。

paragraphs.en: Celan’s influence is tricky to absorb for any poet who did not suffer in a labor camp or witness his parents taken away to be murdered by the S.S. It is impossible to isolate Celan’s aesthetic innovations from his trauma and its historical meanings; Wright preëmpts that bind by presenting his own suffering as sort of lame and clumsy. It keeps him, in these last poems, from putting his finger on the tragedy scale: there are “so many things worse than death,” he writes, and killing, even killing a fly, is one of them. He quotes the haiku master Issa:
paragraphs.zh: 对于任何未在劳改营中受难，或未亲眼见过父母被党卫军带走并杀害的诗人来说，吸收策兰的影响都很艰难。把策兰的美学创新同他的创伤及其历史意义分离开来几乎不可能；赖特则以自己那种近乎生硬、笨拙的苦难呈现来先于此把二者系在一起。正是这种做法，使他在这些末期诗作中不至于把悲剧的尺度轻易点到放大镜下：他写道，“有很多事比死更糟”，而“即便是杀死一只苍蝇”也是其中之一。他引用了俳句大师一茶：

paragraphs.en: Oh why would you swat them, the poor things, forever wringing their skinny hands
paragraphs.zh: 哦，你为何要拍打它们，那些可怜的生灵啊，永远把自己那对瘦骨嶙峋的手紧紧搓成一团

paragraphs.en: Wright’s body is dog shit, after all—don’t kill his companions! “More than ever I am haunted by the lives of the small, brief, powerless, filthy, and poor,” he writes. But “filthy” suggests something less than a Buddhist’s commitment to the flies’ well-being. In other poems, Wright has some fun at the expense of a cricket (“my friend describing you over the phone, chronicling your brief life and fairly gruesome end, both of us guiltily chuckling”) and a moth (“once it observes you have noticed it or given any indication its presence is a nuisance, you will very soon find yourself the perpetual loser in a chronic contest of wills”). We don’t read Wright for the purity of his moral life but for the erratic comedy of his good intentions yielding to blurted reversals and denunciations.
paragraphs.zh: 莱特的身体终究是“狗屎”——别杀了他的同伴！“如今我比以往任何时候都更常被小而短暂、无力、肮脏和贫穷的生命所困。”他说。但“肮脏”这个词暗示的，似乎还少了佛教徒对苍蝇福祉的那种执着。莱特在另一几首诗里也把一些生物当作笑料来玩：像在一首诗里写“我在电话里描述你，记录你短促而相当惨烈的生命与终结，我们两个都带着内疚窃笑”的蟋蟀，以及“飞蛾”（“一旦你察觉到它的存在，或有任何迹象说明它在作乱，你很快就会发现自己在一场长期意志对抗中始终是失败者”）。我们读莱特，不是为了追求他道德生活的纯净，而是为了观赏他那种善意在脱口而出的转折和指责中歪打正着所呈现出的跳跃喜剧。

paragraphs.en: All the unresolved, zigzagging impulses give even Wright’s finished work here some of the raggedness of drafts. “Axe in Blossom” is in every sense an open book, practically an open studio. We see its striking title gestate through multiple phases—in a translation, an original poem, and a collage poem made from lines by Celan, Czesław Miłosz, and Rainer Brambach. There are “Three Homages,” two poems called “The Writing” (the second, marked “2,” is presented first), one poem called “The Lamp, 2” that insinuates an absent predecessor, two called “The Kiss,” and so on; the poems reshuffle the same vocabulary, the same images, the same settings and themes. The volume begins with more or less “complete” work, passes through some abandoned drafts, then trails off into an appendix, “Burial Herbs,” which works like a commonplace book of maxims, partial poems, and poems nominally complete but “unfinishable,” in the sense that certain poems, perhaps the most ambitious ones, only approximate their maker’s vision.
paragraphs.zh: 所有未解决的、之字形摆动的冲动使得莱特在这里的成稿作品也带着几分草稿的毛边感。“Axe in Blossom”在每一层面都近乎一本开放书，几乎是一间开放的创作工作室。我们看到这个醒目的标题在多个阶段生成：先是译文、再到原诗、再到由塞兰、切斯瓦夫·米沃什和莱纳·布兰巴赫诗句拼贴而成的拼贴诗。它包含《三首致敬》（“Three Homages”）、两首名为《The Writing》的诗（标注“2”的那首反而先出现）、一首名为《The Lamp, 2》的诗（它暗示着一个缺席的前作）、两首名为《The Kiss》的诗，等等；这些诗反复改组同一套词汇、同一组意象、同一处景象与主题。该卷首先是或多或少“完整”的作品，接着穿过一些被搁置的草稿，最后过渡到附录“Burial Herbs”，它像一本便签本，收录格言、残句与名义上“写完”但“写不完”的诗——因为有些诗，或许是最雄心勃勃的那些，只能近似于作者最初的构想。

paragraphs.en: Among the “unfinishables” is a poem that I consider a masterpiece, probably one of Wright’s very best. Dedicated to the German poet Karl Krolow, “At His Desk in the Past” was “transcribed from self-recorded drafts on audio,” in 2012. It’s “raining in a dead language” out the window, as Wright measures the duration of his life against various phenomena—some brief, like an infant’s fever, some ancient, like the stars. The poem, written in linked prose blocks, is too long to quote in full, and is quite intricate. But one passage gives a sense of its eerie logic. Wright describes the words of his poem “still traveling toward the world although he is not there although he is not here or anywhere”:
paragraphs.zh: 在这些“写不完”的作品里，有一首我认为是杰作，也许是莱特最优秀的诗之一。它献给德国诗人卡尔·克罗洛夫（Karl Krolow），《在往昔的书桌前》（《At His Desk in the Past》）于2012年“由作者自己录音的草稿转录”而成。窗外“下着死语般的雨”，莱特在其中把自己的一生长度与各种现象作对照——有些短暂，如婴儿的高烧；有些久远，如群星。该诗以相互衔接的散文块写成，太长，无法完整引述，而且结构颇为精巧。但有一段能体现其诡异逻辑：莱特写道，他的诗“仍在朝世界前行，尽管他不在那里、尽管他不在这儿，也不在任何地方”：

paragraphs.en: AND we’d do it again wouldn’t we—every last one of us choosing precisely what we had been given, a glimpse of a night sky—no—choosing precisely what we had been given not to be a sky of infinite diamonds unaware they live forever or exist at all but a pair of mortal eyes with the glory to see them, grieving their loss, a glimpse.
paragraphs.zh: 而我们还会再来一次，不是吗——我们中的每一个人，都精确地选择了自己所被赐予的：一眼看见夜空——不——精确地选择了自己所被赐予的，不是那片无限钻石般的天幕——那样的天幕并不自知自己将永存，或根本存在——而是两只凡人的眼睛，带着看见它们的光辉，因失而悲恸，一抹闪现。

paragraphs.en: Wright welcomes those stars into his own “BRAIN or desert hive this named mind or single roaring.” The poem ends by conferring a childish name, a name that a kid might dream up, on these sad, undying presences, with their “honey-colored irises” and “muted wings” of light and motion: “BEE-iridescent.”
paragraphs.zh: 赖特将那些星辰迎入他自己的“BRAIN or desert hive this named mind or single roaring。”这首诗在诗人将一种孩童般的名字赋予这些忧伤、永不消逝的存在时结束——一种孩子或许会想到的名字：它们拥有“蜂蜜色的虹膜”和“低调的翅膀”，光与运动都在其中闪现：“BEE-iridescent”。

paragraphs.en: Rounding the bend, the book tours Wright’s points of origin: recollections of his first poems, his first trip to Europe, his first cigarette, the first signs of the addictions that so blighted the poet’s life. Wright hears now “the sound of my name posed, in my young mother’s voice before I finally slept.” The poet tucks into his deathbed, its “sheets / the color of old aspirin”:
paragraphs.zh: 转折之处，书中回到赖特的起源：对他最早的诗作、第一次欧洲之行、第一支香烟、以及早期那些几乎毁掉他一生的成瘾迹象的回忆。赖特如今听见“我名字的声音，在我最终入睡前，于我年轻的母亲声音中被提出。”诗人躺在他的临终床上，床单是“旧阿司匹林色”：

paragraphs.en: I had often wondered where I would find it, it find me, or what it would look like. Don’t you?
paragraphs.zh: 我常常想知道，我会在哪里找到它，它会在何处找到我，或者它会是什么样子。你不这样想吗？

paragraphs.en: But, hey, it’s not a deathbed until you die in it: “I could get some work done / here, I shrugged,” Wright decides. “I had done it before.” ♦
paragraphs.zh: 但，嘿，直到你在上面死去，它还算不上临终病床：‘我可以在这里把一些活儿干完，/ 我耸了耸肩，’赖特这样决定。‘我以前也做过这样的事。’♦
